name: schema
context: Design SQLite schemas — CREATE TABLE, column types, constraints, indexes, views, triggers, and ALTER TABLE
info: This skill covers SQLite schema design: creating tables with appropriate type affinities (TEXT, INTEGER, REAL, BLOB, NUMERIC), defining constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK, FOREIGN KEY, DEFAULT), creating indexes for query performance, building views for reusable queries, writing triggers for automated actions, and using ALTER TABLE for schema evolution. SQLite uses a flexible type system based on type affinity rather than strict types — any column can store any value unless STRICT mode is enabled. Understanding type affinity, rowid behavior, and indexing strategy is essential for building correct and fast schemas. Agents use this skill when creating new databases, adding tables, designing data models, and evolving schemas. See [[sqlite/queries]] for reading and writing data, [[sqlite/performance]] for index optimization, and [[sqlite/extensions]] for virtual tables like FTS5.
triggers: add a foreign key in sqlite
add a column to sqlite table
body:
# SQLite Schema Design

SQLite schema design differs from other databases in important ways: flexible type affinity, implicit rowid, and limited ALTER TABLE. This skill covers everything agents need to create correct, performant schemas.

## CREATE TABLE

```sql
-- Basic table
CREATE TABLE users (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,           -- alias for rowid (auto-increment)
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  age INTEGER CHECK(age >= 0),
  bio TEXT DEFAULT '',
  created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
  updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

-- With foreign key
CREATE TABLE posts (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT NOT NULL,
  body TEXT NOT NULL,
  author_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  published INTEGER DEFAULT 0,       -- boolean (0/1)
  created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

-- STRICT mode (enforce type checking)
CREATE TABLE measurements (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  sensor_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  value REAL NOT NULL,
  recorded_at TEXT NOT NULL
) STRICT;

-- IF NOT EXISTS (safe for idempotent scripts)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS migrations (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  applied_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
);

-- WITHOUT ROWID (for composite primary keys or small tables with text keys)
CREATE TABLE settings (
  key TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  value TEXT NOT NULL
) WITHOUT ROWID;
```

## Type Affinity

SQLite uses **type affinity** rather than strict types. Any column can store any value (unless STRICT).

| Affinity | Preferred Types | Use For |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| `INTEGER` | integers | IDs, counts, booleans (0/1), Unix timestamps |
| `TEXT` | strings | names, emails, ISO-8601 dates, JSON, UUIDs |
| `REAL` | floats | prices, measurements, coordinates |
| `BLOB` | raw bytes | files, images, binary data |
| `NUMERIC` | any numeric | flexible numeric storage |

### Boolean Convention

SQLite has no BOOLEAN type. Use `INTEGER` with values `0` (false) and `1` (true):

```sql
CREATE TABLE features (name TEXT, enabled INTEGER DEFAULT 0 CHECK(enabled IN (0, 1)));
```

### Date Convention

SQLite has no DATE type. Store as:
- **TEXT** `'2026-04-11 12:00:00'` — human-readable, sortable, use datetime() functions
- **INTEGER** `1744358400` — Unix epoch, compact, use strftime()
- **REAL** `2460775.0` — Julian day number, for astronomical calculations

Recommendation: Use TEXT with ISO-8601 format and `DEFAULT (datetime('now'))`.

## PRIMARY KEY and ROWID

```sql
-- INTEGER PRIMARY KEY is an alias for the implicit rowid
CREATE TABLE items (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
-- id and rowid are the same column

-- AUTOINCREMENT prevents rowid reuse (rarely needed)
CREATE TABLE logs (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, message TEXT);

-- Composite primary key
CREATE TABLE post_tags (
  post_id INTEGER REFERENCES posts(id),
  tag_id INTEGER REFERENCES tags(id),
  PRIMARY KEY (post_id, tag_id)
);

-- UUID primary key (use WITHOUT ROWID for efficiency)
CREATE TABLE events (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT (lower(hex(randomblob(16)))),
  type TEXT NOT NULL
) WITHOUT ROWID;
```

## Constraints

```sql
CREATE TABLE products (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  sku TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  price REAL NOT NULL CHECK(price > 0),
  stock INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK(stock >= 0),
  category TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'general',
  -- Table-level constraints
  UNIQUE(category, name)
);
```

## Foreign Keys

```sql
-- IMPORTANT: Foreign keys are OFF by default!
PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
  total REAL NOT NULL,
  FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE
    ON UPDATE CASCADE
);

-- ON DELETE actions: CASCADE, SET NULL, SET DEFAULT, RESTRICT, NO ACTION
```

## Indexes

```sql
-- Single column index
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

-- Composite index (column order matters for queries)
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_author_date ON posts(author_id, created_at DESC);

-- Unique index (alternative to UNIQUE constraint)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email_unique ON users(email);

-- Partial index (index only matching rows)
CREATE INDEX idx_active_users ON users(email) WHERE active = 1;

-- Expression index
CREATE INDEX idx_users_lower_email ON users(lower(email));

-- Covering index (includes all columns the query needs)
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_covering ON posts(author_id, created_at, title);
```

## Views

```sql
-- Reusable query
CREATE VIEW active_users AS
SELECT id, name, email, last_login
FROM users
WHERE active = 1;

-- Use like a table
SELECT * FROM active_users WHERE last_login > datetime('now', '-7 days');

-- Drop and recreate (no ALTER VIEW)
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS active_users;
```

## Triggers

```sql
-- Auto-update updated_at timestamp
CREATE TRIGGER users_updated_at
AFTER UPDATE ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  UPDATE users SET updated_at = datetime('now') WHERE id = NEW.id;
END;

-- Audit log trigger
CREATE TRIGGER orders_audit
AFTER INSERT ON orders
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, row_id, action, timestamp)
  VALUES ('orders', NEW.id, 'INSERT', datetime('now'));
END;

-- Prevent deletion trigger
CREATE TRIGGER prevent_admin_delete
BEFORE DELETE ON users
FOR EACH ROW WHEN OLD.role = 'admin'
BEGIN
  SELECT RAISE(ABORT, 'Cannot delete admin users');
END;
```

## ALTER TABLE

SQLite has limited ALTER TABLE support:

```sql
-- Add a column (always allowed)
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone TEXT;

-- Rename a table
ALTER TABLE old_name RENAME TO new_name;

-- Rename a column (SQLite 3.25.0+)
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN phone TO phone_number;

-- Drop a column (SQLite 3.35.0+)
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_field;
```

For other schema changes (change types, modify constraints), use the **12-step ALTER TABLE procedure**:

```sql
-- 1. Create new table with desired schema
CREATE TABLE users_new (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE
  -- removed: old_column
  -- changed: email is now UNIQUE
);
-- 2. Copy data
INSERT INTO users_new (id, name, email) SELECT id, name, email FROM users;
-- 3. Drop old table
DROP TABLE users;
-- 4. Rename new table
ALTER TABLE users_new RENAME TO users;
-- 5. Recreate indexes, triggers, views
```

## Schema Inspection

```sql
-- List all tables
SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name;

-- Show CREATE statement for a table
SELECT sql FROM sqlite_master WHERE name='users';

-- List columns
PRAGMA table_info('users');

-- List indexes
PRAGMA index_list('users');

-- Show index columns
PRAGMA index_info('idx_users_email');

-- List foreign keys
PRAGMA foreign_key_list('orders');

-- Check foreign key violations
PRAGMA foreign_key_check;
```

sections.title: CREATE TABLE
sections.body: Define tables with columns, type affinities, and constraints. Use INTEGER PRIMARY KEY for auto-incrementing rowid alias. Use IF NOT EXISTS for idempotent creation. Use STRICT mode for type enforcement. WITHOUT ROWID is optimal for tables with TEXT primary keys or composite primary keys with no need for rowid access.

sections.title: Type affinity system
sections.body: SQLite has five type affinities: INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB, NUMERIC. Unlike other databases, SQLite allows any value in any column unless STRICT mode is used. The declared type maps to an affinity via rules: 'INT' → INTEGER, 'CHAR/TEXT/CLOB' → TEXT, 'REAL/FLOAT/DOUBLE' → REAL, 'BLOB' → BLOB, everything else → NUMERIC. Store booleans as INTEGER (0/1), dates as TEXT (ISO-8601), and JSON as TEXT.

sections.title: Index design
sections.body: Create indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, and GROUP BY. Composite indexes must match the leftmost prefix of query conditions. Partial indexes (WHERE clause) reduce index size. Expression indexes enable indexed lookups on computed values. Covering indexes include all columns a query needs, avoiding table lookups. Use [[sqlite/performance]] and EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN to verify index usage.

sections.title: Foreign keys
sections.body: Foreign keys are OFF by default — always run `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON` when opening a connection. Define with REFERENCES and specify ON DELETE/ON UPDATE actions: CASCADE (propagate), SET NULL, SET DEFAULT, RESTRICT (prevent), NO ACTION (defer check). Use PRAGMA foreign_key_check to find violations in existing data.

sections.title: Views and triggers
sections.body: Views are named SELECT statements. Use for reusable queries, security (expose subset of columns), and simplification. Triggers fire on INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — BEFORE or AFTER. Access NEW.column and OLD.column in trigger bodies. Use RAISE(ABORT, message) to reject operations. Common patterns: auto-updating timestamps, audit logs, cascading denormalized fields.

sections.title: ALTER TABLE and migrations
sections.body: SQLite ALTER TABLE supports: ADD COLUMN (always), RENAME TABLE, RENAME COLUMN (3.25+), DROP COLUMN (3.35+). For other changes, use the 12-step procedure: create new table, copy data, drop old, rename new, recreate indexes/triggers/views. Wrap migrations in transactions. Track applied migrations in a migrations table.
